title: Universalities of Defects in Quantum Field Theories
abstract: Defects are both physically rich objects and powerful tools in modern quantum field theory. They are extended operators, such as boundaries, impurities, and probe particles, embedded in many-body systems. In this dissertation, we study the universal aspects of defect dynamics from the perspective of symmetry principles. We bring together several themes, including defect renormalization group flows, effective string theory, and impurities in atomic quantum gases.
summary: This work explores how the long-distance behavior of defects in quantum field theories is governed by universal symmetry principles. It establishes effective field theory descriptions for complex objects like chiral defects and giant vortices in superfluids. The results show that microscopic details are often irrelevant to the macroscopic dynamics of these systems.
main_problem: Investigating the universal aspects of defect dynamics in quantum field theories, specifically focusing on how symmetry principles determine the infrared properties of boundaries, impurities, and vortices.
main_result: Demonstrates that defect dynamics (such as chiral defects in Maxwell theory and giant vortices in superfluids) are universally determined by symmetry, identifying new effective theories like Warped Conformal Field Theory (WCFT) for rotating superfluids.
method: Uses symmetry-based analysis, Renormalization Group (RG) flows, Operator Product Expansion (OPE), and effective field theory approaches including the epsilon expansion.
model_or_system: The work covers a variety of models including Maxwell theory with topological duality defects, O(N) models, and non-relativistic systems like atomic quantum gases and rotating 2D superfluids.
key_observables: Scaling dimensions, conformal weights, bulk-to-defect OPE coefficients, displacement and tilt operators, and particle density profiles.
important_parameters: UV and IR limits, codimension, conformal weights, angular velocity, and the large angular momentum limit (giant vortex regime).
main_assumptions: Assumes translation symmetry perpendicular to the defect is broken and that scaling invariance is enhanced to conformal symmetry at fixed points.
figures_summary: Visuals include configurations of various defects, RG and phase diagrams, correlation functions, and physical profiles of particle density and eigenmodes.
paper_structure: The dissertation progresses from a general framework of defect dynamics in QFT to specific applications in Maxwell theory, O(N) models, and finally to non-relativistic systems like quantum gases and rotating superfluids.
why_interesting: It provides a deep connection between high-energy symmetry principles (like CFT and duality) and the emergent universal dynamics in many-body systems like ultracold atomic gases and superfluids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2.1: The configuration of the conformal twist defect and the topological duality defect in R4. The vertical red line is the 2-dimensional twist defect, which is localized at w = ¯w = 0 and extends along the (z, ¯z) plane. The twist defect is attached to the 2- dimensional topological defect DN shown as the half-infinite red plane. This figure only displays the R3 slice defined by z = ¯z.

Figure 2.2: DCFT two-point and three-point functions. The red plane represents the twist defect at w = ¯w = 0. Left: the bulk-defect two-point function ⟨F ˆOs ℓ⟩. Middle: the bulk- defect-defect three-point function ⟨F ˆOs2 ℓ2 ˆOs3 ℓ3⟩in the limit ξ →0, where the bulk point x approaches the defect. Right: the three-point function ⟨F ˆOs2 ℓ2 ˆOs3 ℓ3⟩in the limit ξ →+∞, where the projection of the bulk point onto the defect plane coincides with the midpoint between (z2, ¯z2) and (z3, ¯z3).
Figure 2.2. It follows from the monodromy condition (2.37) that ⟨F ˆOs2 ℓ2 ˆOs3 ℓ3⟩= 0 unless s = s2 +s3 ∈ Z ± 1

Figure 2.3: The configuration of the anyonic brane (AN)k and the twist defect. The red circle denotes the twist defect defined on a Lorentzian cylinder. The red disk denotes the topological duality defect DN. The anyonic brane is shown as a blue circle, which links with the twist defect. The magnetic 1-form symmetry operator ηm(α) is shown as the blue surface. This figure only shows the spatial R3, and every defect except for ηm(α) extends in the time direction.

Figure 2.4: IR-stable fixed points associated with bilinear defect deformations in the free theory. The blue curve shows how the fixed point depends on the spacetime dimension d, while the grey dashed arrow indicates the defect RG flow from the UV to the IR. Fixed points with physical interpretations in integer dimensions are marked in red.
